case_name:
15.         The  Director  submitted  that  the  facts  established  that 
there  was  professional misconduct both under both section
100(1)(a) and
100(1)(b) of the HPCA Act. Referring to authorities she relied onMartin v
Director of Proceedings3and the rejection by the
Court of Appeal ofPillai v Messiter4as raising
“too high a threshold” and relied rather onF v Medical
Practitioners Disciplinary Tribunal.5

summary.brief:
15.         The  Director  submitted  that  the  facts  established  that 
there  was  professional misconduct both under both section
100(1)(a) and
100(1)(b) of the HPCA Act. Referring to authorities she relied onMartin v
Director of Proceedings3and the rejection by the
Court of Appeal ofPillai v Messiter4as raising
“too high a threshold” and relied rather onF v Medical
Practitioners Disciplinary Tribunal.5. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: The Charge alleged malpractice or negligence or conduct which brought discredit to the medical profession under the HPCA Act
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal is of the view that the Charge is not made out in respect of any of its Particulars and it is dismissed.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Dr Love’s evidence also was that he had never heard of surgical stimulation in a patient with an adequate regional anaesthetic causing leg movement.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr Love

evidence_index.text: Mrs S described her relief after the baby had been delivered, having been exhausted and crying and “ in a lot of pain. ”
evidence_index.speaker: Mrs S

evidence_index.text: There is a significant element of judgment call about Dr N’s response to the situation as he perceived it.
evidence_index.speaker: Tribunal

evidence_index.text: The conclusion that the Tribunal has reached is that there is insufficient evidence that there were non-verbal indicators of pain or verbal complaints of pain
evidence_index.speaker: Tribunal

evidence_index.text: The baby was delivered at 6.44 p.m, that is 4 minutes after the skin incision which Dr A described as acceptable and reasonable incision delivery interval.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr Jones said that, based on the first blood pressure record at 6.22 p.m. and the top-up of epidural at 6.24 p.m.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr Jones

evidence_index.text: Ms Mitchell said that just before the operation commenced Ms Johnson noticed Mrs S’s legs move and she saw that too;
evidence_index.speaker: Ms Mitchell

evidence_index.text: The Charge against him and each of its three Particulars are therefore dismissed.
evidence_index.speaker: Tribunal

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Mrs S was initially planned for a vaginal delivery but due to non-reassuring CTG trace, it was decided to proceed with a caesarean section.

requestables.name: Anaesthetist's assessment
requestables.description: Assessment of patient's pain and anaesthesia level

reveal.result_summary: Patient reported discomfort, anaesthetist assessed as manageable
reveal.clinical_significance: Assessment influenced decision not to alter anaesthetic plan

timeline_phases.name: Caesarean Section
timeline_phases.duration: 4 minutes for delivery, 45 minutes for suturing

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient experiencing pain during caesarean section
decision_points.prompt: What should the anaesthetist do in response to the patient's pain?

options.clinical_reasoning: To manage patient's pain effectively during surgery

actual_action_defendant.description: Anaesthetist continued with current anaesthetic plan

expected_action_court.description: Additional anaesthesia should have been administered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence suggested inadequate pain management

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Pain management was inadequate as per expert testimony
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Additional anaesthesia or analgesia should have been considered

key_findings: The Tribunal finds that there has not been such misconduct on Dr N’s part.
The Charge against him and each of its three Particulars are therefore dismissed.

factual_timeline.event: Decision to proceed with caesarean section

court_findings.finding: The Tribunal finds that there has not been such misconduct on Dr N’s part.
court_findings.legal_significance: No breach of standards requiring disciplinary sanction
